descriptionParagraphs: Operation Dark Heart (2010) - oop is a memoir by Army Reserve Lt. Col. Anthony Shaffer. Martin's Press, in conjunction with the DoD created a second, redacted edition; which contains blacked out words, lines, paragraphs, and portions of the index.

What makes it interesting is the way a book becomes legible to officials as a political instrument rather than a neutral cultural object. As a memoir, it can be read not only for subject matter but for the way form, tone, and circulation make a text feel dangerous, intimate, or politically usable to anxious officials.

It also matters as part of a wider censorship history in United States. The present page is a dossier starter built from source-tracked ban records; the surviving note currently says: In September 2010, the United States Department of Defense (DoD) overrode the Army's January approval for publication. The DoD then purchased and destroyed all 9,500 first edition copies, citing concerns that it. More publication history, translations, and close reading can be added later.

banningParagraphs: Operation Dark Heart (2010) - oop entered censorship debates as a memoir associated with politics, public argument, and state power. In the current dossier, the main state objections cluster around political control and political dissent.

counterReadings.title: The Origins of Totalitarianism
counterReadings.author: Hannah Arendt
counterReadings.note: A foundational analysis of state terror, propaganda, and ideological conformity.

counterReadings.title: On Tyranny
counterReadings.author: Timothy Snyder
counterReadings.note: A short modern guide to resisting authoritarian politics and controlled public discourse.

counterReadings.title: 100 Banned Books: Censorship Histories of World Literature
counterReadings.author: Nicholas J. Karolides, Margaret Bald, and Dawn B. Sova
counterReadings.note: A compact reference on how censorship systems moved across states, churches, and courts.

counterReadings.title: Banned Books: 387 B.C. to 1978 A.D.
counterReadings.author: Anne Lyon Haight
counterReadings.note: Useful for comparing older obscenity, heresy, and political bans with modern free-speech disputes.
